message.content: in @apps/cli/src/lib/claude-settings.ts why is it if I run `rudel enable` in the root of this repository, that the hook is added to my user directory? It shuold NEVER be added to the user directory. only to the current project/repo

content.thinking:
The user is asking about why the `rudel enable` command adds the hook to the user's global `~/.claude/settings.json` instead of the project-specific settings. Let me look at the code to understand the flow.

The issue is in `getClaudeSettingsPath()` - it always returns `~/.claude/settings.json` (the global user settings), but it should be writing to the project-level settings file instead.

Claude Code supports project-level settings at `.claude/settings.json` within the project directory. Let me trace the code to understand the full flow.

content.text:
The issue is in `getClaudeSettingsPath()` on line 26 — it hardcodes the path to the global user settings (`~/.claude/settings.json`):

```ts
return join(process.env.HOME ?? "~", ".claude", "settings.json");
```

It should instead resolve to the **project-level** settings file (`.claude/settings.json` relative to the repo root). Let me check how `enable` is invoked to understand the full picture.
The fix is straightforward. `getClaudeSettingsPath()` needs to resolve the **project-local** `.claude/settings.json` (relative to the git repo root) instead of the global `~/.claude/settings.json`.
No existing git-root helper. I'll add one and update the settings path to use it.

Now let me check how Claude Code project settings work — the project-level file is `.claude/settings.json` at the repo root. I also need to ensure the `.claude/` directory is created if it doesn't exist.

toolUseResult.stdout:
$ bun build src/bin/cli.ts --outdir dist --target bun
Bundled 39 modules in 43ms

  cli.js  137.23 KB  (entry point)

content.text:
Builds cleanly. Here's what was wrong and what changed:

**Bug**: `getClaudeSettingsPath()` was hardcoded to `~/.claude/settings.json` (the **global user** settings), so `rudel enable` always wrote the hook to your user-wide config regardless of which repo you ran it from.

**Fix**: It now uses `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` to find the current repo root and writes to `<repo>/.claude/settings.json` (the **project-level** settings file). It also creates the `.claude/` directory if it doesn't already exist.
